title: FDI vs FPI: Impact on Indian Markets
focus: Conceptual differences between Foreign Direct Investment and Foreign Portfolio Investment, and their respective impacts on capital markets and the Rupee.
rationale: This is a foundational macroeconomic concept comparing two major types of foreign investment, highly testable for UPSC prelims conceptual questions.
sectionExcerpt: FDI involves long-term strategic investment in primary markets (real assets, >10% stake), providing stable capital and steady Rupee impact. FPI involves short-term financial investment in secondary markets (stocks/bonds, <10% stake), causing high capital market volatility (hot money) and Rupee fluctuations.
evidenceBullets: FDI is a long-term commitment involving primary market entry with stakes greater than 10 percent.
FDI provides stable capital market foundations and steady Rupee inflows without causing daily volatility.
FPI is a short-term financial investment in secondary markets with stakes less than 10 percent.
FPI acts as hot money, driving market volatility and causing sharp Rupee fluctuations requiring RBI intervention.

title: Foreign Direct Investment (FDI) Framework and Trends
focus: FDI governance, entry routes (Automatic vs Government), prohibited sectors, and recent inflow statistics.
rationale: FDI rules, prohibited sectors, and top source countries/sectors are recurring themes in UPSC exams.
sectionExcerpt: India's net FDI inflows fell by 96% in FY25 despite a rise in gross FDI. FDI is governed by the FEMA Act (1999) and the Consolidated FDI Policy (2020). About 90% of FDI is under the Automatic Route (e.g., Agriculture, Telecom, Insurance). The Government Route requires prior approval. FDI is prohibited in lottery, gambling, chit funds, Nidhi companies, atomic energy, and railway operations. Top FDI source countries in 2024-25 were Singapore (30%), Mauritius (17%), and the USA (11%), with the Services sector receiving the highest share (19%).
evidenceBullets: Net FDI is the difference between inward FDI flows and outward FDI flows (repatriation plus outward FDI by Indian firms).
Approximately 90 percent of FDI in India is under the automatic route, which does not require prior RBI or Central Government approval.
FDI is strictly prohibited in lottery businesses, gambling, chit funds, Nidhi companies, atomic energy, and railway operations.
In 2024-25, the top three source countries for FDI inflows were Singapore, Mauritius, and the USA.
The Services sector received the highest share of FDI inflows (19 percent) in 2024-25.

title: Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) and Reclassification
focus: Regulatory limits on FPI holdings and the RBI/SEBI framework for reclassifying FPI to FDI.
rationale: The 10% equity cap and the new 2024 reclassification framework are specific regulatory updates highly relevant for economy questions.
sectionExcerpt: FPIs can hold a maximum of 10% of an Indian company's total paid-up equity capital. If they exceed this cap, they must either divest the surplus shares or reclassify as Foreign Direct Investment (FDI). The 2024 RBI and SEBI framework states that reclassification is not permitted in FDI-prohibited sectors. Furthermore, FPI investments requiring government approval (especially from land-bordering countries) need the Indian Investee Company's concurrence for reclassification.
evidenceBullets: FPIs are restricted to holding a maximum of 10 percent of an Indian company's total paid-up equity capital.
Upon exceeding the 10 percent cap, FPIs have the option to divest surplus shares or reclassify their investment as FDI.
The 2024 RBI and SEBI framework prohibits FPI to FDI reclassification in sectors where FDI is already prohibited.
Reclassification requires government approval for investments from land-bordering countries and mandates the concurrence of the Indian Investee Company.

title: Internationalization of the Rupee and SRVA
focus: Special Rupee Vostro Accounts (SRVAs) and their role in rupee-based trade settlements.
rationale: Vostro accounts and Rupee internationalization are critical current affairs topics in the external sector.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI has removed prior approval requirements for banks to open Special Rupee Vostro Accounts (SRVAs) to speed up rupee-based trade settlements and support the internationalization of the Indian Rupee. The SRVA mechanism, introduced in July 2022, enables exporters and importers to invoice and settle trade in Indian Rupees. A Vostro Account refers to a foreign bank's account held in a domestic bank in the local currency.
evidenceBullets: RBI removed the prior approval requirement for banks to open Special Rupee Vostro Accounts (SRVAs).
The SRVA mechanism allows exporters and importers to invoice and settle international trade in Indian Rupees.
A Vostro Account is defined as a foreign bank's account held in a domestic bank denominated in the local currency.
